section_1.section_instruction: Identify which familiar policy frames appear in the text. More than one frame can be present at the same time.

annotation_1.name: Humanitarian frame
annotation_1.tooltip: Tick if the text emphasizes protection, vulnerability, dignity, rights, or humanitarian obligation.
annotation_1.example:
Text:
"Aid groups said the reception system must protect families from further harm."

Response:
{"response": true}

annotation_2.name: Economic frame
annotation_2.tooltip: Tick if the text emphasizes labour supply, costs, fiscal pressure, or economic contribution.
annotation_2.example:
Text:
"Employers argued that visa restrictions are worsening labour shortages."

Response:
{"response": true}

annotation_3.name: Security frame
annotation_3.tooltip: Tick if the text emphasizes crime, trafficking, border control, disorder, or public safety.
annotation_3.example:
Text:
"Ministers said stronger border checks were needed to disrupt trafficking networks."

Response:
{"response": true}

section_2.section_instruction: Choose the main frame that organizes the text overall.

annotation_1.name: Primary frame
annotation_1.tooltip: Choose the dominant frame. Use mixed if multiple frames are equally prominent, and no clear frame if none stand out.
annotation_1.example:
Text:
"The candidate linked migration to crime while also warning about welfare costs."

Response:
{"response": "mixed"}

section_3.section_instruction: Capture the wording that best supports your frame judgement.

annotation_1.name: Evidence
annotation_1.tooltip: Provide a short phrase or sentence from the text that best supports your coding decision.
annotation_1.example:
Text:
"Volunteers described the programme as a humane response that helps families rebuild their lives."

Response:
{"response": "a humane response that helps families rebuild their lives"}
